content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
January27, 1998
David H. Choate
870 W. Henderson Avenue
Porterville, California 93257
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-625
Dear Mr. Choate:
This letter responds to your request for advice about the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”))
We cannot advise you at this time because we do not have enough facts to give
meaningful advice about this situation.
Based on your advice request (a copy ofwhich is
attached) and our subsequent telephone conversations, it is unclear whether the situation you
describe presents a conflict-of-interest question under the Act. Please note carefully that this
does not mean we have concluded that you do not have a conffict; neither have we concluded that
you do. Although we may discern that the situation revolves around litigation concerning the
proposed drainage reservoir, without more information about the litigation (or threatened
litigation), the parties, and the nature of the governmental decisions the Council will have to
make, we cannot proceed.
Given that we cannot answer your first question for the reasons explained above, it is
premature to respond to your second question.
Finally, there is some indication in your advice request that the City Attorney excluded
you from the December 2d closed session, and perhaps intends to exclude you from future closed
sessions, based on an attorney-client privilege theory, not on the basis of a purported conflict of
interest on your part. The Commission is authorized to advise only about the Act. We are unable
to advise you about this apparent aspect of the situation becaue it is outside our jurisdiction.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Conmiission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-625
Page 2
We encourage you to resubmit your request if additional facts become available. In the
meantime, I have enclosed a pamphlet explaining the Act’s conflicPof-interest rules, including
participation in closed sessions. Perhaps this pamphlet will help you understand the questions
you must ask to begin resolving this situation. If you have any other questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
SGC:JV:tls
Legal Division
I:\Advice Ltrs\97625.wpd

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city council member has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act regarding litigation concerning a proposed drainage reservoir, and related questions about participation in closed sessions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot provide advice at this time because insufficient facts were provided to determine whether a conflict-of-interest question exists under the Act. The Commission is also unable to advise on the attorney-client privilege issue, which falls outside its jurisdiction.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No separate QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections found in document; both synthesized from letter body. This is a declination/incomplete response letter where FPPC cannot advise due to lack of facts. File number appears twice with slight variation (1-97-625 and A-97-625).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a city council member has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act regarding litigation concerning a proposed drainage reservoir, and related questions about participation in closed sessions.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC cannot provide advice at this time because insufficient facts were provided to determine whether a conflict-of-interest question exists under the Act. The Commission is also unable to advise on the attorney-client privilege issue, which falls outside its jurisdiction.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declines to advise on a request regarding potential conflicts of interest related to drainage reservoir litigation and closed session participation, citing insufficient factual information and jurisdictional limitations regarding the attorney-client privilege aspect.
